rewrites.newText: Her mother, who kept insisting the move would be brilliant, had ordered her to sort them
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail misattributed to the wrong character
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage shows Aisha unpacking her own room; her mum gives no such instruction.

rewrites.newText: She wanted to be able to find any particular book quickly once homework started at her new school
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-literal practical reading true-in-world but unsupported
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The text frames the alphabetising as emotional comfort, not efficiency or homework.

rewrites.newText: Because the word relentless is a gentle, soothing term describing a naturally warm and patient mother
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong definition of the vocabulary word
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Relentless means unceasing and hard to stop, not gentle; it signals forced cheer.

rewrites.newText: Because her mother repeated the phrase deliberately to irritate Aisha and wear down her objections to moving
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 2 says her mum was trying to convince herself, not deliberately annoy Aisha.

rewrites.newText: Because her mother had always been a naturally cheerful person, long before the family ever decided to move
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The cheerfulness is recent and effortful, not a lifelong natural disposition.

rewrites.newText: Because her mother was genuinely delighted about the move to the new town and could not hide her joy
rewrites.targetsMisreading: surface reading that reverses the author's point
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'Relentless' plus 'trying to convince herself' means the happiness is forced, not genuine.

rewrites.newText: Quiet, reserved, and too shy to approach new people
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Meg introduces herself unprompted and chatters constantly — the reverse of quiet and shy.

rewrites.newText: Popular and sporty, endlessly competing to win at everything
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Meg is artistic, not sporty; paragraph 11 says the group were not the most popular.

rewrites.newText: Nervous and careful, easily upset whenever anything goes slightly wrong
rewrites.targetsMisreading: trait belonging to a different character
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This describes Aisha, not Meg, whose unselfconscious enthusiasm shows confidence.

rewrites.newText: They all share a deep love of art and painting murals together
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong shared interest generalised from one detail
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Only Meg works on the mural; Omar likes chess, Priya poetry, Lucas Rubik's cubes.

rewrites.newText: They are all very similar, sharing the same hobbies and the same personalities
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The friends have deliberately varied, distinct interests; the point is individuality.

rewrites.newText: She gradually stops noticing the uniform altogether, which shows that her new friends have distracted her so completely that school itself no longer matters
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible-sounding inference the text never licenses
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The blazer changes from a 'costume' to feeling like it 'belongs to her' — active belonging, not indifference.

rewrites.newText: She begins customising and decorating the blazer to express her individuality, which shows that her growing confidence has made her want to stand out
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage never says Aisha alters the blazer; the change is internal.

rewrites.newText: She adores the maroon blazer from the very first morning, which shows that she was genuinely excited about starting at her new school all along
rewrites.targetsMisreading: no-change reading with the wrong starting point
rewrites.wrongnessReason: At the start the uniform felt like a costume for a part she had not auditioned for — dread, not love.

rewrites.newText: She hates the stiff new uniform at the beginning and goes on hating it right to the end, which shows that she never truly settles in
rewrites.wrongnessReason: By paragraph 11 the blazer feels like it belonged to her and she does settle in.

rewrites.newText: The struggles that parents face when they move their children to a completely new town
rewrites.targetsMisreading: minor characters mistaken for the theme
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage centres on Aisha's own experience of finding belonging; the parents are background.

rewrites.newText: How becoming skilled at painting and art can help a nervous child succeed at school
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Art is merely the setting; Aisha cannot paint and the story is about friendship and belonging.

rewrites.newText: The idea that moving house is always a serious mistake that families should try hard to avoid
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage ends positively with Aisha settled and happy, not presenting the move as a mistake.

rewrites.newText: Sam is practical and hard-working, while Jess is lazy and unwilling to make any real effort
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Jess is energetic and drives the adventure — the opposite of lazy.

rewrites.newText: Sam loves being outdoors on adventures, while Jess would always rather stay safely at home indoors
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Jess is eager for the outdoor adventure; Sam is the reluctant one, so preferences are swapped.

rewrites.newText: He had completely forgotten the cave existed in the many years since discovering it
rewrites.wrongnessReason: His journal note 'Must return with proper equipment' shows he clearly remembered.

rewrites.newText: The passage sets out his exact reasons clearly and in careful, complete detail
rewrites.targetsMisreading: meta non-answer that dodges the actual reason
rewrites.wrongnessReason: This gives no reason at all, and the text only implies the cause through a brief note.

rewrites.newText: He was frightened of the dark, unexplored passage and never dared to venture inside
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He was a professional geologist who mapped caves; the note attributes the delay to lacking equipment.

rewrites.newText: He was secretly hoping to sell the valuable cave to the government for a large sum
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage never mentions selling; he intended to return himself with proper equipment.

rewrites.newText: The cave was formed only very recently, in the last few years, by fast-moving water that is still carving the rock
rewrites.wrongnessReason: 'Thousands of years ago' signals great age, contradicting a recent formation.

rewrites.newText: Water still flows regularly through the cave today, which is exactly what made the rock beneath their feet feel so smooth
rewrites.targetsMisreading: present-tense misreading of a past process
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The water had long since found a different route and the passage is cool and dry.

rewrites.newText: The floor is dangerous because the constant water has left it wet and slippery, making it very easy for the children to fall
rewrites.targetsMisreading: hazard detail borrowed from the wrong location
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The slippery surface was the ledge outside; inside, the passage is explicitly cool and dry.

rewrites.newText: She was afraid that someone hiding deeper inside the cave might overhear them and discover them
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The whisper expresses awe at the beauty; no one else is present and the passage conveys wonder.

rewrites.newText: Sam had firmly told her to keep her voice down so they would not attract attention
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Sam gives no such instruction; the whisper is Jess's own spontaneous reaction of reverence.

rewrites.newText: By bribing him with sweets and promising to share her pocket money afterwards
rewrites.wrongnessReason: No bribe is offered; she persuaded him by appealing to what Grandpa Arthur would have wanted.

rewrites.newText: By threatening to go into the dark cave alone if he refused to come with her
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Jess uses no threat; her persuasion is emotional, invoking their grandfather's wishes.

rewrites.newText: By promising him that exploring the cave would be completely safe and free of any real danger
rewrites.wrongnessReason: She never claims it is safe; both know it is risky.

rewrites.newText: By telling him that their parents had already given full permission for the two of them to explore
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 7 says it was exactly the sort of thing their parents would forbid.

rewrites.newText: He has completely overcome every one of his fears and now feels no anxiety whatsoever about any kind of danger
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The 'sensible thing' still pulls at him; he remains cautious, so his fears are moderated, not erased.

rewrites.newText: He feels a heavy sense of duty to finish the exploration that his grandfather began but was never able to complete
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He continues because the wonder makes the sensible option unpersuasive — willingness, not felt duty.

rewrites.newText: He has become completely reckless and no longer cares at all about the danger, throwing away the caution that once defined him
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He folded the map and tucked it back — a measured act; he stays cautious but becomes braver.

rewrites.newText: It makes the cave sound dangerous and unstable, as though the heavy stone formations might suddenly collapse onto the children
rewrites.targetsMisreading: danger reading of a beauty description
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The vivid detail evokes wonder and beauty, not menace or instability.

rewrites.newText: It proves that the author must have personally visited a great many real caves in order to describe them so accurately
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The question asks about effect on the reader; vivid writing shows craft, not the author's travels.

rewrites.newText: She has never learned Morse code and cannot read it at all
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 5 says she taught herself Morse and knew enough to recognise the pattern was not Morse.

rewrites.newText: Morse code can only be sent using sound, never using flashes of light
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Morse can be signalled by light; her reason is the pattern matches no Morse character.

rewrites.newText: She thinks Morse code is far too old-fashioned for anyone to be using now
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Her stated reason is the pattern does not correspond to a standard Morse character, not the code's age.

rewrites.newText: The flashing signals appeared far too slowly to possibly be genuine Morse code
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Speed is never the issue; the mismatch is the pattern maps onto no Morse character.

rewrites.newText: She does not care in the slightest where she lives, feeling equally at home anywhere
rewrites.wrongnessReason: She plainly cares — she had not entirely settled and leans on her hobbies to cope.

rewrites.newText: She absolutely loves the village and has already made a large circle of close, loyal friends there
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The other children treat her with polite wariness and she has not settled.

rewrites.newText: She hates living in Pencarrow so much that she is desperate to move straight back to her old home
rewrites.wrongnessReason: She copes through her passions and never expresses hatred or a wish to move back.

rewrites.newText: She finally manages to decipher a hidden Morse code message that spells out the name of the mysterious signaller
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage ruled out Morse; the breakthrough is realising the pattern is music.

rewrites.newText: She works out exactly who has been climbing the lighthouse each night to send the mysterious flashing signals across the bay
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage ends before she learns who; decoding the melody is the breakthrough, not identifying the sender.

rewrites.newText: To make the passage much longer so that it fills more space on the page
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The gradual reveal is a deliberate suspense device, not filler.

rewrites.newText: Because the author simply forgot to include all of the details together earlier in the story
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The staggered pacing is intentional craft to build suspense, not an oversight.

rewrites.newText: To test whether the reader is paying close enough attention to remember all the earlier clues
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The purpose is to build tension and mirror the step-by-step investigation, not test memory.

rewrites.newText: Because Noor herself was confused by the pattern and needed a long time to think it all through
rewrites.targetsMisreading: character state confused with authorial choice
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The question asks about the author's structural decision; Noor's confusion is a plot detail.

rewrites.newText: To deliberately frustrate and annoy the reader by refusing to give them any answer
rewrites.wrongnessReason: A cliffhanger creates intriguing anticipation, the opposite of deliberate frustration.

rewrites.newText: Because the author has not yet decided who is sending the signals from the lighthouse
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The withholding is a deliberate cliffhanger technique, not a sign the author is unsure.

rewrites.newText: Because the identity of the person sending the signals does not really matter to the story
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The sender's identity is the central mystery driving Noor's quest, so it clearly matters.
